concepts.name: Steel Mini-Mills
concepts.definition: Low-cost steel producers using electric arc furnaces, initially able to make lower-quality steel such as rebar but eventually improving into bars, structural steel, and sheet.

concepts.name: What Goes Up, Can't Go Down
concepts.definition: Firms are pulled upmarket toward higher margins and better customers; once cost structure and profit expectations rise, moving downmarket becomes unattractive or impossible.

concepts.name: Performance Oversupply
concepts.definition: As technologies improve faster than mainstream customers need, lower-cost entrants can become good enough and then move upward.

arguments.claim: Integrated mills rationally ceded low-end markets and thereby opened the path for mini-mills.
arguments.evidence: Rebar was low-margin and unattractive to integrated mills, while mini-mills had lower cost structures and could profit there before improving into higher grades.

arguments.claim: Upmarket migration is economically natural for entrants and incumbents alike.
arguments.evidence: Mini-mills climbed from rebar to bar, structural, and sheet as each tier became technically feasible and more profitable.

cases.name: Nucor mini-mill disruption
cases.context: Nucor and other mini-mills began in rebar and gradually attacked higher-value steel categories.
cases.illustrates: The low-end foothold and upmarket trajectory at the heart of disruption.
cases.lesson: A market leaders gladly exit can become the disruptor's training ground.

logicChains.description: Mini-mills enter rebar → integrated mills exit low-margin tier → mini-mills improve and attack bar → incumbents retreat again → entrants climb to structural and sheet → incumbents lose core profit pools.
logicChains.sourceHint: Steel mini-mill story and performance-demand framing.

terminology.definition: Low-end reinforcing bar market where mini-mills first gained foothold.
Traditional high-cost steel producer optimized for broad/high-grade production.
A disruption that starts with overserved customers or unattractive low-end segments and moves upward.

nuance: Ceding low-margin business can be correct locally and disastrous strategically.
The disruptor's cost structure is as important as its technology.
